summary: Martin Fowler announces his retirement from public speaking, revealing that despite being a successful conference keynote speaker with hundreds of thousands of YouTube views, he experiences severe anxiety and panic attacks before every talk. He describes physical symptoms including racing heart, chest tightness, and overwhelming urges to flee. While he's managed these feelings professionally for years, the COVID pandemic's break from speaking made him realize he doesn't want to return to that stress. He plans to redirect his energy toward writing and helping colleagues communicate their ideas through articles on his website.
key_points: Despite natural comfort with public speaking as a young person, Fowler developed severe pre-talk anxiety that includes panic attack symptoms
The anxiety doesn't just occur before talks but can strike days earlier, affecting sleep and work, and even manifests while writing about the topic
Once on stage, adrenaline typically takes over within minutes, though online talks lack this compensating effect
Fowler feels fortunate to have reached a career point where he can decline speaking without significant consequences
His future focus will be on writing and editorial work, helping colleagues publish their insights on martinfowler.com
money_quotes: I far prefer a trip to the dentist to have fillings done.
The prospect of a future of frequent speaking was the only thing that's seriously made me consider leaving Thoughtworks.
Before I go on stage I get intense feels of dread, an overwhelming desire to flee - to lock myself in a toilet or lose myself in some strange city.

After all my father worked in a factory that ruined his lungs, countless people take serious health risks in their daily work - compared to that I don't feel I can complain too much about these bursts of anxiety.
Certainly my experience has given me an understanding of why so many people in the performing arts struggle with drug addictions.
I've reached the point in my life where I'm lucky enough to be able to avoid things that make me miserable, and want to take advantage of my good fortune.
key_insight: Professional success and public visibility don't immunize you from anxiety, and reaching a point where you can decline work that makes you miserable is a privilege worth exercising.
